> **Goal:** turn equations into pictures. Plot functions, data, surfaces,
> contours, and vector fields so that abstract formulas become intuition.

## Why a mathematician should care

You already read graphs fluently on paper. Matplotlib lets you draw the same
graphs from a formula in seconds, and — crucially — for functions and datasets
far too messy to sketch by hand. In machine learning we constantly look at loss
*surfaces*, gradient *fields*, and data *distributions*; a good picture is
often the fastest path to understanding what an algorithm is doing.

## The core recipe

Almost every plot of a function follows three steps:

1. **Grid the input** with `np.linspace(a, b, n)` (or `np.meshgrid` in 2D).
2. **Vectorise the formula**: `y = np.sin(x)` computes all outputs at once.
3. **Draw and decorate**: `plt.plot(x, y)`, then title / labels / legend / grid,
   and finally `plt.show()`.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### Notebook 02a — Plotting functions & data
- `np.linspace` to make a smooth grid of $x$ values.
- `plt.plot` to draw a curve; `plt.title`, `plt.xlabel`, `plt.ylabel`,
  `plt.grid`, `plt.legend` to decorate it.
- **Multiple curves** on one axes, with **line styles & colors**
  (`"r--"`, `linestyle`, `linewidth`).
- `plt.scatter` to show raw data points instead of a line.
- `plt.subplots(rows, cols)` to place several panels in one figure.
- `plt.hist` to reveal the **shape of a distribution**.
- `plt.bar` for category comparisons (e.g. class probabilities later on).
- `plt.fill_between` to shade a region, and `plt.annotate` to label a point.

### Notebook 02b — Surfaces, contours & vector fields
- `np.meshgrid` to build a 2D grid, then `Z = f(X, Y)` for the whole grid.
- `plt.contour` / `plt.contourf` — level curves and filled "topographic" maps.
- `ax.plot_surface` with `projection="3d"` — a true 3D surface
  (uses `mpl_toolkits.mplot3d`).
- `plt.quiver` — a **vector field**, e.g. the gradient $\nabla f$.
- `plt.imshow` + `plt.colorbar` — a matrix or any 2D array as a heatmap.

## Two ways to talk to Matplotlib

| Style | Looks like | Good for |
|-------|------------|----------|
| pyplot (stateful) | `plt.plot(...)`, `plt.title(...)` | quick single plots |
| axes (object) | `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`, `ax.plot(...)` | subplots, 3D, control |

Both appear in this chapter; they do the same thing on the "current" plot.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| Nothing shows | you forgot `plt.show()` (or are not in a notebook) |
| Curve looks jagged | too few points in `np.linspace` — use more |
| `meshgrid` shape | `Z = f(X, Y)` is 2D, one value per grid point |
| Too many arrows | use a **coarse** grid for `quiver`, fine for `contour` |
| Labels overlap | call `fig.tight_layout()` |
| `contour` vs `contourf` | lines vs filled bands (the **f** = *filled*) |

## After this chapter

You'll be ready for **Chapter 03 — Calculus & Gradients**, where these contour
and quiver pictures become the language for derivatives, gradients, and the
gradient-descent algorithm at the heart of training.

## 📚 Further reading

- [Matplotlib documentation](https://matplotlib.org/stable/) — full plotting reference and gallery.
- [Mathematics for Machine Learning](https://mml-book.com) — Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong (free PDF) — the math behind the pictures.
